steps.title: Korean Food
steps.desc: Food vocabulary — and every dish name is a compound you can decode. 밥 is the most important word in Korean: it means both 'rice' and 'meal' and even functions as 'How are you?'
goals: Understand why 밥 is culturally central

steps.exampleEn: Have you eaten? (lit: Did you eat rice?)
steps.note:
밥: rice AND meal AND food in general.

밥 먹었어요? is how Koreans say 'How are you?'
Literally: 'Have you eaten rice?'

Korean culture: food = care.
Asking if someone ate = asking if they're OK.

국: thin soup (lighter, usually served with rice).
찌개: thick stew (heartier, shared at center of table).

된장찌개: soybean paste stew
김치찌개: kimchi stew
순두부찌개: soft tofu stew

찌개 always goes at the center. Everyone shares it.

COMPOUND: 불 (fire) + 고기 (meat).
Literally: 'fire meat.'

고기 alone means meat in general:
소고기 (beef), 돼지고기 (pork), 닭고기 (chicken)

COMPOUND: 비빔 (mixing) + 밥 (rice).
Literally: 'mixed rice.'

Rice + vegetables + fried egg + gochujang.
Mix everything vigorously before eating!
비비다: to mix. 비빔: mixed.

COMPOUND: 김치 + 찌개.
찌개 reappears in: 된장찌개, 순두부찌개.

김치찌개 is the most common home-cooked Korean dish.
Every Korean family has their own recipe.

steps.exampleEn: Cold noodles are delicious in summer.
steps.note:
COMPOUND: 냉 (cold/冷) + 면 (noodles/麵).

냉: cold. Also in 냉장고 (refrigerator = cold+storage+box).
면: noodles. Also in 라면 (ramen).

평양냉면 vs 함흥냉면: two famous regional styles.

라면: ramen / instant noodles (Korean-style).

Korea is the world's #1 per-capita ramen consumer.
신라면, 불닭볶음면 are famous globally.

면 again! Same building block as 냉면.

COMPOUND: 떡 (rice cake) + 볶이 (stir-fried).

볶이: stir-fried (from 볶다: to stir-fry)
볶음밥: fried rice = 볶음 + 밥

떡볶이 is Korea's #1 street food.
떡 reappears in: 떡국 (rice cake soup, New Year's)

steps.title: 🍚 밥 먹었어요? — More Than Food
steps.text:
한국어 문화 TIP:

밥 먹었어요? ('Have you eaten?')

This is how Koreans greet each other.
It's NOT literally asking about food.
It means: 'Are you doing OK? Are you taking care of yourself?'

If someone asks this:
→ 네, 먹었어요. / 아직 안 먹었어요.
   (Yes, I ate. / I haven't eaten yet.)

Refusing a meal someone offers = refusing their care.
Accepting food from an elder = accepting their love.

밥 is not just food in Korean culture.
밥 is intimacy, care, and connection.

steps.q: 밥 먹었어요? literally means 'have you eaten?' but is used as:

steps.s: ___이 먹고 싶어요. (I want to eat ramen)

steps.hint: I like Korean food. Especially bibimbap is delicious.

steps.q: 면 appears in both 냉면 and 라면. 면 means:

steps.title: Please Give Me ___
steps.desc: 을/를 marks the OBJECT of a sentence — the thing the verb acts on. This completes the core particle trio you've been building. Today: using it to order food and request things.
goals: See the full particle trio (은/는, 이/가, 을/를)

steps.note:
을/를 marks the OBJECT — the thing being acted on.

를 after vowel: 커피를, 차를, 물을...
Wait: 물 ends in consonant ㄹ → 물을

Rule: 를 after vowel-ending, 을 after consonant-ending.
커피 (ends in vowel ㅣ) → 커피를
밥 (ends in consonant ㅂ) → 밥을

steps.en: 을 or 를: vowel vs consonant
steps.note:
CONSONANT ending → 을:
밥을, 김치찌개를 아니라... 김치찌개를!
김치찌개 ends in vowel ㅔ → 를
밥 ends in consonant ㅂ → 을
라면 ends in consonant ㄴ → 라면을

Test: what is the LAST letter of the word?
Consonant → 을 / Vowel → 를

주세요: please give.
[thing]을/를 주세요: '[thing], please.'

주다: to give.
주세요: 주 + 세요 (polite request).

메뉴 주세요: Menu, please.
물 주세요: Water, please.
영수증 주세요: Receipt, please.

You now have the CORE PARTICLE TRIO:

은/는: TOPIC ('as for X...')
이/가: SUBJECT (the doer or what 'is')
을/를: OBJECT (what the verb acts on)

저는 커피를 마셔요:
저는: 'as for me' (topic)
커피를: coffee (object — what I'm drinking)
마셔요: drink (verb — last!)

Full name and contrast: Unit 5.

steps.title: 🎯 을 vs 를: The Fast Test
steps.text:
ONE quick test:

Look at the LAST letter of the word.
Is it a consonant (받침)?
→ Add 을

Is it a vowel (no 받침)?
→ Add 를

Examples:
밥 (ㅂ = consonant) → 밥을
커피 (ㅣ = vowel) → 커피를
라면 (ㄴ = consonant) → 라면을
비빔밥 (ㅂ = consonant) → 비빔밥을
불고기 (ㅣ = vowel) → 불고기를
냉면 (ㄴ = consonant) → 냉면을

After 50 sentences, your mouth does this automatically.

steps.q: 물___ 주세요. (물 ends in consonant ㄹ)
steps.hint: 물 has 받침 ㄹ → consonant ending → 을

steps.q: 커피___ 주세요. (커피 ends in vowel ㅣ)
steps.hint: 커피 has no 받침 → vowel ending → 를

steps.q: 비빔밥___ 먹고 싶어요. (비빔밥 ends in ㅂ)

steps.hint: 을/를 = object particle. What is being eaten/given/etc.
I eat rice and drink coffee — both are objects
Topic + Subject + Object = the three core particles

steps.title: I Want To ___
steps.desc: Your first verb chain beyond the basics. 먹고 싶어요 has FOUR pieces snapped together. This is how Korean builds meaning — suffix by suffix. Notice the pattern. Unit 14 gives it a name.

steps.note:
Pattern: verb STEM + 고 싶어요

먹다 → 먹고 싶어요 (want to eat)
가다 → 가고 싶어요 (want to go)
보다 → 보고 싶어요 (want to see)
마시다 → 마시고 싶어요 (want to drink)
하다 → 하고 싶어요 (want to do)

Step 1: drop -다 from dictionary form
Step 2: add -고 싶어요

steps.exampleEn: I don't want to eat spicy things.
steps.note:
Negative of -고 싶어요:
-고 싶다 → -고 싶지 않아요

먹고 싶지 않아요: don't want to eat
가고 싶지 않아요: don't want to go

Or more casual: -고 싶지 않아요 → -고 싶어요 + 아니요

steps.en: What do you want to eat?
steps.example: A: 뭐 먹고 싶어요? B: 비빔밥 먹고 싶어요.
steps.exampleEn: A: What do you want to eat? B: I want bibimbap.
steps.note:
THE most common mealtime question in Korea:

뭐 먹고 싶어요?
→ What do you want to eat?

뭐 할 거예요?
→ What are you going to do?

뭐가 좋아요?
→ What do you like?

뭐 = what (casual short form of 무엇)

steps.exampleEn: What is it? / What are you doing?
steps.note:
뭐 = what (casual spoken form of 무엇).

뭐예요?
→ What is it?

뭐 해요?
→ What are you doing?

뭐 먹어요?
→ What are you eating?

뭐가 좋아요?
→ What do you like?

뭐 is one of the most common words in Korean.

steps.title: 🚂 Verb Trains: Your First Glimpse
steps.text:
Look at 먹고 싶어요 closely:

먹 (eat) + 고 (connector) + 싶 (desire) + 어요 (polite)

먹고 싶어요.
→ I want to eat.

Korean builds meaning by STACKING pieces on the verb stem:

먹고 싶지 않았어요.
→ I didn't want to eat.
(eat + connector + want + negative + past + polite)

By Unit 14 this system gets its formal name.
For now: notice that each suffix adds one layer of meaning.

steps.q: 먹고 싶어요 has how many pieces?

steps.hint: Add 지 않아요 to make the negative

steps.q: A: 뭐 먹고 싶어요? What is being asked?
opts: How much food do you want?
What time do you want to eat?
What do you want to eat?
steps.ans: What do you want to eat?
steps.hint: 뭐 = what, 먹고 싶어요 = want to eat

steps.s: 한국에 ___고 싶어요. (I want to go to Korea)
steps.hint: 가다: to go → stem is 가

steps.s: 비빔밥을 먹고 ___. (I want to eat bibimbap)

steps.hint: What do you want to eat? I want to eat bulgogi.

steps.q: 하고 싶어요 comes from which verb?
'I want to see a Korean drama':
steps.hint: 보다 (to see) → 보고 싶어요, and 드라마를 (object)

steps.en: to talk / to chat / to tell a story
steps.note:
이야기하다: to talk, to chat, to tell a story.
이야기 (story/conversation) + 하다 (do).

친구하고 이야기해요: chatting with a friend.
재미있는 이야기: fun/interesting story.

말하다 vs 이야기하다:
말하다: to speak/say (general)
이야기하다: to talk/chat/tell stories (conversational)

steps.title: Delicious or Not?
steps.desc: Taste vocabulary. 맛있다 is a compound you can already decode. Korean adjectives are actually verbs — they conjugate. The system is the same one you're already using.
goals: See Korean adjectives conjugated as verbs

steps.note:
COMPOUND: 맛 (taste) + 있다 (exist).
Literally: 'taste exists' = delicious.

맛있어요: polite present form.

정말 맛있어요!: Really delicious!
너무 맛있어요!: So delicious! (너무 = so/too much)

COMPOUND: 맛 (taste) + 없다 (not exist).
Literally: 'taste doesn't exist' = tasteless/bad.

맛있다 ↔ 맛없다. Same LEGO logic.

Warning: don't say this about food someone cooked!
맛이 좀 특이해요 (the taste is a bit unique) is more polite.

맵다 (to be spicy) → 매워요 (polite form).
⚡ JRTF: 맵다 changes shape slightly.

안 매운 거 있어요?: Is there something not spicy?
조금 매워요: It's a little spicy.
많이 매워요: It's very spicy.

달다 → 달아요: sweet
쓰다 → 써요: bitter
짜다 → 짜요: salty

떫다 → 떫어요: astringent (tannic — unripe fruit, tea)
고소해요: nutty/savory (sesame, nuts)
새콤해요: tangy/sour

맛 vocabulary is core for restaurant conversation.

SINO-KOREAN: 음 (eat/飮) + 식 (food/食).

음식: food in general (any food).
음식점: restaurant (food-eating-place).
식사: meal (eat-food, more formal).

음 reappears in: 음료 (beverage = drink+flow), 음악 (music)

steps.title: 🌶️ Korean Food: The Five Tastes
steps.text:
Korean cuisine is famous for layered flavor.
The five traditional tastes:

단맛 (sweet/단): desserts, sweet potatoes
신맛 (sour/신): vinegar, kimchi
쓴맛 (bitter/쓴): coffee, bitter melon
짠맛 (salty/짠): soups, kimchi
매운맛 (spicy/매운): gochujang, kimchi

Han-sik (한식/Korean food) uniquely combines all five.

Useful restaurant vocab:
맛이 어때요?
→ How does it taste?

맛있어요!
→ Delicious!

맛없어요.
→ Not tasty.

좀 달아요.
→ It's a bit sweet.

많이 매워요.
→ It's very spicy.

steps.q: 'I don't want to say the food is bad' — polite alternative to 맛없어요:
steps.hint: 맛이 좀 특이해요 = 'the flavor is a bit unique' — polite

steps.s: 이 음식은 너무 ___! (This food is so delicious!)

steps.hint: Kimchi is spicy. And it's salty.

steps.title: At a Korean Restaurant
steps.desc: Real restaurant interaction from arrival to paying. You know all the pieces — now put them together in a full dialogue.

steps.note:
저기요!
→ Excuse me! (literally: hey, over there!) — standard call to staff.

여기요!
→ Hey, over here! (also used, slightly less common)

저기요 is the universal safe default.

주문: order (noun).
주문하다: to order (verb).

주문 받으세요?: 'Ready to take our order?'
주문할게요: 'I'll order.' (proactive)

주문이요: 'Order!' (calling for staff to take order)

steps.exampleEn: Could I have some water, please?
steps.note:
좀 = a bit. Softens any request.

주세요.
→ Please give me.

좀 주세요.
→ Please give me (more polite).

좀 makes you sound more Korean. Use it constantly.

계산: calculation/bill.
계산해 주세요: 'Please do the calculation' = Check please.
계산해 주실 수 있어요?: 'Could you take care of the bill?'

계산서 주세요: 'Please give me the bill.'

In Korea: often pay at the counter, not tableside.

포장: packaging/wrapping.
포장이요 or 포장해 주세요: 'To go, please.'

먹고 갈게요: 'I'll eat here.'

Delivery (배달) is HUGE in Korea — many Koreans
never enter the restaurant at all.

steps.hint: 주문하다 = to order; 할게요 = I will
좀 softens and polishes any request

steps.s: ___요! 주문할게요. (Calling the waiter, ready to order)
steps.hint: 저기요: standard way to call a waiter

steps.s: 비빔밥 ___ 주세요. (Soften the request: bibimbap please)
다 먹었어요. ___ 해 주세요. (We're done. Check please.)
저기요! 비빔밥 {1} 주세요. 그리고 물도 {2} 주세요.
steps.hint: One bibimbap please. And some water too.

steps.q: In Korea, how do you usually pay?
steps.hint: Korean restaurants typically pay at the counter, not tableside
계산 = calculation; 해 주세요 = please do for me

steps.title: Side Dishes, Sharing, and the 빨리빨리 Spirit
steps.desc: Korean dining culture. Side dishes are free and infinite. Sharing is mandatory. Speed is expected. This lesson is about understanding the social rules that make Korean dining unique.

steps.note:
반찬: side dishes (always plural).

Always FREE. Always refillable.
Ask for more anytime:
반찬 좀 더 주세요: More side dishes, please.

더: more. 좀 더: a little more (politer).

더 = more.

물 좀 더 주세요.
→ More water, please.

반찬 좀 더 주세요.
→ More side dishes, please.

조금만 더.
→ Just a tiny bit more.

더 attaches to any request.

같이: together.
같이 먹어요!: 'Let's eat together!'

Korean dining is fundamentally communal.
개인 접시 ✗: no individual plates (usually)
공유 접시 ✓: shared dishes at the center

잘 먹겠습니다: said before eating
잘 먹었습니다: said after eating (formal thanks for meal)

빨리빨리: 'hurry hurry!' / 'quickly quickly!'

This is a CORE Korean cultural concept.
빨리빨리 culture = Korean speed culture:
• Food arrives in 3-5 minutes (not 20)
• Eating is fast (30-40 min max)
• Turnover is expected
• No lingering after the meal

빨리: quickly / fast. Doubled for emphasis.

steps.title: 🥢 Korean Dining: The Rules
steps.text:
Dining etiquette matters. Here's what you need to know:

어른 먼저: Elders eat first.
Don't pick up chopsticks before the oldest person.

공유: Everything is shared from the center.
Don't order 'my plate' and 'your plate.'

반찬 무한 리필: Side dishes are FREE and refillable.
Asking for more = completely normal.

잘 먹겠습니다: Said BEFORE eating.
잘 먹었습니다: Said AFTER eating.

계산: Oldest/host usually pays.
Splitting bills (더치페이) is becoming common among young people
but still unusual at formal meals.

빨리빨리: Don't be surprised by how fast everything moves.

steps.hint: 반찬 무한 리필 — unlimited free side dishes
어른 먼저: elders eat first — Korean age hierarchy
빨리빨리 = hurry hurry — Korea's pace of doing things fast

steps.s: 물 ___ 더 주세요. (A little more water please)
steps.hint: 좀 더 = a little more (polite softener)
Before eating, then ask for more side dishes
먹었 is PAST tense — after the meal

steps.title: At the Market
steps.desc: Fruit, vegetables, and grocery vocabulary. You'll use counters from U3 and 얼마예요 from U3 — this is the first time you're pulling earlier lessons into a new context.

steps.note:
과일: fruit (general term).

수박=수(water)+박(gourd): watermelon
딸기: strawberry (note: double ㄸ = tense)
포도: grape
복숭아: peach
망고: mango (Konglish)

사과: apple
배: pear (also = stomach/belly!)
딸기: strawberry (double ㄸ)

사과 = apologize (same word!)
사과할게요: I'll apologize.
사과 주세요: Apple, please. / Apology, please!
Context makes it clear.

steps.exampleEn: I eat a lot of vegetables.
steps.note:
채소: vegetables (general term).

양파: onion (양 = foreign/Western + 파 = green onion)
마늘: garlic (native Korean)
당근: carrot (native Korean)
오이: cucumber (native Korean)

Korean cuisine uses garlic and onion in almost everything.

신선하다: to be fresh.
신선해요: it's fresh.

신선: SINO-KOREAN: 신(new/新) + 선(fresh/鮮).
신선한 채소: fresh vegetables
신선하지 않아요: not fresh

오늘 들어왔어요?: 'Did this arrive today?' (freshness check)

steps.exampleEn: How much for one pack of strawberries?
steps.note:
Review from Unit 3:
얼마예요?: How much is it?
이거 얼마예요?: How much is this?
딸기 한 팩에: for one pack of strawberries

한 팩: one pack
한 봉지: one bag
한 kg: one kilogram

steps.hint: 양파 = Western green onion = onion (introduced from outside Korea)

steps.s: 딸기 두 ___ 주세요. (2 packs of strawberries)
steps.hint: 팩 = pack — container counter for packaged items

steps.s: 채소를 ___ 먹어요. (I eat a lot of vegetables)
steps.hint: 많이 = a lot, in large quantity
Two apples and one pack of strawberries

steps.q: 신선해요 = 신선하다 conjugated. 신선하다 comes from:

steps.title: Unit 4 Complete
steps.desc: Food compounds, the object particle, wanting things, taste words, restaurant culture, and shopping. All vocabulary from Units 1-4 only.

steps.exampleEn: I want to eat bibimbap. It's delicious!
steps.note:
OBJECT PARTICLE: 을/를 (consonant/vowel)
WANTING: -고 싶어요 (stem + 고 싶어요)
FOOD COMPOUNDS: 불고기, 비빔밥, 떡볶이 decoded
TASTE: 맛있다, 매워요, 달아요, 짜요, 써요
CULTURE: 반찬, 빨리빨리, 어른 먼저

steps.hint: 반찬 무한 리필 — this is Korean dining culture
커피 ends in vowel → object particle 를
비빔밥 ends in consonant ㅂ → 을

steps.s: 한국에 가고 ___. (I want to go to Korea)
이 음식이 ___. (This food is spicy — use conjugated form)
steps.hint: 맵다 → 매워요 (JRTF flag — irregular but just memorize it)
I want to eat bibimbap. Please give me water.

steps.q: 빨리빨리 culture means Korean restaurants are:
opts: Fast — food arrives and people eat quickly
steps.ans: Fast — food arrives and people eat quickly
steps.hint: 빨리빨리 = hurry hurry = speed in everything
